transcript: Good morning, it's Sunday the 11th of May, we're gonna go for a drive at sunrise around the Nauru. Worldwide trip, to the ends of the earth and back. I don't think this was originally intended to be a place to park your car. Pretty sure it wasn't. But, like with most most things in the room, order is seldom maintained and you can pretty much do what you want, as long as that doesn't include fixing things, doing regular maintenance, or picking up garbage. That's not allowed. I tried to have a meal last night in the hotel restaurant because I didn't really want to go out. But the restaurant was closed for a private event so I have no idea what the hotel manager thought their guests would do for dinner if they weren't fortunate enough to have a vehicle. Drive through the Menin Hotel. Entrance, only see all these temporary containers. 24 container type arrangements. A lot of the staff working at the refugee camps at the top of the hill are out on the street. So we are now driving counter-clockwise around. Let's see how long it takes us. The speed limit here is, for some strange reason, well not strange, but it's 40 kilometers an hour, and I find that sometimes a little bit difficult. I'm going to keep sane, but it's a bit above 60. This up on the right is the country's only recreational arena. I don't think there is water at the moment the bottom of time, I did see a Korean flag sailed in there. Pretty nice one, for a Korean flag. Not enough. See, encounter a homecoming. Ah, stray dogs, yes, that is a problem in the room, and from time to time, they attack you. They stick it together in packs and revert to their instinctive behavior and start roaming and hunting and they get aggressive and territorial. Two nights ago, I understand, a six year old boy was hauled to death by feral dogs That's really sad It seems like something could be done about the feral dogs It would take any kind of organization to call them Let's see what the unaffected agenda is It's supposed to be pretty nice, it's nice if you're driving clockwise and keep looking right. It's really not that bad. some places are nice, like in much of the Pacific, the exceptions, as far as I've found, are Cook Islands, Fiji perhaps, there doesn't seem to be a lot of investment or interest in maintaining things or improving things, certainly. Very little, and I don't know as what that can be attributed to, if there's any one factor. You can see that the power company has replaced a few of their rusting steel poles with new wooden poles that should last 20 years or so, pressure treated, imported from New Zealand. It's important as it that not all the poles that were imported from New Zealand for this purpose actually made it to the power plant. They did make it to the power plant, but they let out the back door for the purposes of building the deck at one of Garu's only two night spots as far as I'm aware, that being Jules by the Bay, and the other night spot being the Menin Hotel, where occasionally, as we know, the restaurant is closed for private events. You can tell guests wondering where they might be their evening meal. Not to worry. I didn't survive. I didn't see dinner. Which I didn't in any case. I went to what is reputed to be the best restaurant I don't survive the same dinner, which I didn't in any case I went to what was reputed to be the best restaurant in town In fact, that's where I ate every single night this week Not by... not on purpose, not my taste But that's where people said they didn't want to go That restaurant is called The Bay We're past that We have about a minute 2 of this video on the left hand side See, this person observes the speed limit Although it's not posting it, but I'm just trying to word out that it's going to be a little bit speed limitous So we're doing 4 kilometers an hour, so we're ready to go. Can't do it. 60 I can handle. Crab on the road, trying to get across. Lots of crabs. You won't see them on the menu when you push from there. I don't think we have lots of crabs here. And when it rains, lots and lots of bubbles. Evaporate after a couple of days. I don't see the drain away. You can make little big puddles, which slows things down a bit. Traffic jams. Racing through the puddles. I think we're coming up on......Tropelis, which is......it's the largest import-export trading company in Peru. They have basically a general store, a hardware store, lots of containers, you can see. And actually some accommodations on the second floor there called the Iwa Lodge It's Capelli and Partner Not partners, just one partner He's a silent partner We don't know who he is, he's just a partner For that matter, I don't know who Capelli is As far as I'm aware, that place is owned by a family called Oppenheimer. Not the same Oppenheimer that was the father of our nuclear program. Not that guy. All of it is in the Pacific. We get a lot of testing in the Pacific, he did not decide to make an order to his home, he never cared before. I'm sure the Pacific was originally there at one point, colonized by Germany. I guess maybe they date from that era. Um, we should have to look it up. Maybe I should have cleaned the windshield before I started out on this to give me a better view. At least that works. I see a lot of the cars on this island, including the one I'm driving in, don't have license plates. Some do. Those that do, that's completely voluntary. Arbitrary. People just create their own vanity plates and put them on their cars. So what that tells us is that there really is no vehicle registration, you know. Although, I'm coming away from Nauru with an interesting souvenir, which is a Nauru driving license. Not a lot of people have those, I'm sure. The last time I was here is when I paid my fee to get a new normal driving license. But the machine that prints the driving licenses wasn't working at the time. So I was issued a piece of paper that said I'd be in defeat. I was considered a licensed driver. I didn't have to take a test. I didn't have to do anything. I just had to pay $16 Australian dollars. I could show them a license from whatever other jurisdiction. I'm sure they don't care. They just want $15. And away I went. Wow. It sure does rain here. Yeah, I guess I could have, well, drew there at full speed and got a car wash. I'm kind of worried about the car stalling up. I don't want that to happen. I have a feeling we're about halfway through our journey. Let's see. Stuff's maintained. It looks like at one point it was built to be nice, somebody's pride. Brand new shop, brand new house, brand new this and that. A few coats of paint make it look a whole lot closer to new, but that doesn't seem to be on the priority list. Lots and lots of gas stations. A lot of them are no longer functioning. So I understand that that was like in the heyday of the ruling there was a wash with cash from there, phosphate royalties, that was what people did all the time, is fill up their vehicles and drive around like I'm doing right now on this beautiful Sunday morning. Just in case you're wondering, it's just after 7 o'clock in the morning. The sun just rose over the sea. It's like anywhere. Not remarkable, but pretty. Coming up onto the Nauru Hospital here in a minute on the left side. Which a few months ago had a big fire and lost all of the health records of the population, alright. That's tough. There was maybe birth certificates as well. Pretty soon, on the right, if you keep an eye out, you'll see Nauru Utilities Corporation, which is responsible for power, production, and water, and handling fuel supplies, diesel, power generation, and all the vehicle fleet, marine and aviation. And they run a few reverse osmosis plants to create fresh water. At one time, they had an operative that used to waste heat from the exhaust power generators, but they moved away from that for some reason. This is the only bridge, as far as I'm aware. It's not a bridge over a river, but there is a river here, but there is this conveyor belt that brings phosphate from the crushing mills onto the loading docks to be loaded onto ships and taken away to market. and locally among at least the Australians who've come to call Nauru their temporary home while they work in camps or whatnot, they call the bridge the Sydney Harbour Bridge. come to call Nauru their temporary home while they work in camps or whatnot. They call the bridge the Sydney Harbour Bridge, which I thought was kind of tongue-in-cheek. The lack of a better name, I guess. It's the Odin Iwo Hotel on the right, and I'm just passing. It's the fallback hotel for people, I guess, when they come to Nauru. And somehow they have more vacancy than the Menin Hotel, which is where I'm staying. Which, believe me, is far from a lot of luxury. On the left is the Civic Center. There's a couple of shops, this and that. And I guess something to do with government. And we're pulling up onto the airport now. I'll go ahead and turn left here. You can also turn right around the runway. But I'll take the shortcut. And drive right past the terminal, which is about 30 seconds. The runway is, as far as I can tell, exactly 2,000 meters long, which I guess allows you You can get pretty decent sized planes in and out of here. I saw a large plane take off the other day, not quite sure what kind of plane it was, it was a large four engine wide body plane that converted for cargo use. It's going to beat it. and the bird from Carpe Diem I made it Here on the left is the airport That's the terminal Not a lot to it Planes come in here seldom enough that they let people go out and play soccer on the, well, on the runway. A lot of people actually go out there for their evening walks precisely because of the stray dog problem. So I'm told. These guys in front of me are speeding. We're going at 50 kilometers an hour. This is a convoy of ball breakers, regular strike. Great, there's reckless driving. Believe me folks, we are almost back at the minute. It doesn't take long. I hope my narration hasn't annoyed you too much. but it would be better than a silent drive around an island. That wouldn't have been very informative. So yeah... I'm driving nice and slow again. There we go. There's Milton and Ross on the right, a great place to avoid having breakfast, I did it once So I'll just stick to the Manatee Hotel, at least there you can get something like French toast Eggs and bacon toast, baked beans, English style, it's not bad. Lot of scrap metal on this island. If they took you up in the interior, you'd be amazed. There's a gold mine, scrap metal. somebody could just organize a collection and ship it away. Lots and lots of big cast iron chunks of metal from the mining operations at the old power plant. Lots of cars on blocks that are rusted out. If we'd driven around the other way, you probably would've seen a circa 1975 Lincoln Continental sitting in somebody's front yard, just rusting away. Might've been a pretty car back in the day when it was new I don't know what I'm counting on Probably didn't see it It was kind of hidden hidden on the paths that you could never see it. That happened. Let's see what else. There's not a lot more I can tell you. I guess if you wade out into the sea from here, you'll find that the floor goes out about maybe 100 meters, and then there's the reef that circles the island. And beyond that, the sea drops pretty quickly to some pretty deep bottom. You know, not too far out there you're at a kilometer or more deep. Walk out there barefoot, which I did once, you'll find the sand is really silty, really really silty. That may be because of all the dust that was created from the mining operations, just my guess. It kind of blows around the island, everything gets kind of mixed up all the time. They may have washed off also into the sea somehow or just accumulated. But on a positive note, there's plenty of fish around, it's not a given that the restaurant will actually worry about its supply chain and make sure it has good fish on the menu, that's certainly not the case. But when you get it, the sashimi is really nice, different sorts of tuna. Different sorts of tuna. Back by the airport, there's a place. It's a mom and pop fish shop. It has a... It has a chalkboard out there. Advertising what they've caught is available. They had some sort of snapper, some bonita, some yellowtail the other day. I didn't stop in to have a look, but I'm sure it was as advertised. But from there to the bay restaurant is probably all of maybe five kilometers. Less than a quarter around the island, for sure. But somehow, none of that fish ended up on the Bay Restaurant's menu. I should say, they have it on the menu. They have sashimi. But the entire week I was here, the only time they had sashimi was the first day. and remember I told you I just ate there every night, so that was seven days, and there's the Menin Hotel, we are back where we started, and that, folks, is Nauru, just over 20 minutes. There you go. Let's see here. Can I get back to my room? That, on the right here, is a pinnacle, a limestone pinnacle, and the roof has the mining economy that is focused on the phosphate. And when you mine out the phosphate, what's left between the phosphate deposits are these limestone pitfalls. And that's what those are. And we're done. I'm turning this off. Hope you've enjoyed during the route with me. Bye.
